data.title: Subprime borrowers are having trouble paying loans, widening the wealth gap in America – Moody’s Zandi
data.description:
Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody’s Analytics, is sounding the alarm on a troubling economic indicator: subprime borrowers are increasingly struggling to keep up with their loan payments.

As of February, more than 10% of debt owed by borrowers with credit scores below 660 at origination was delinquent, which Zandi noted is the “highest delinquency rate in more than a decade.”

 

 

 

Despite the concerning trend, the economist said he is “not particularly worried about what this means for the financial system.”

According to Zandi, subprime debt levels are significantly lower than before the Global Financial Crisis, when $3.5T in subprime debt represented about 30% of total debt outstanding. Today, subprime debt stands at $2.7T, accounting for only 15% of total outstanding debt.

What worries Zandi more is what these numbers reveal about the financial health of lower- and middle-income Americans. Even with an unemployment rate of just 4.4%, these borrowers are struggling to make their payments while facing $4-per-gallon gas prices—up a dollar from a month ago—along with higher interest rates and little prospect for refinancing their debts.

“I shudder to think what will happen if unemployment continues to rise,” he said.

The strategist emphasized that how subprime borrowers are faring serves as a “reliable leading economic indicator, signaling troubled times” ahead.

These warning signs emerged even before the economic fallout from hostilities in the Middle East, suggesting the financial strain on everyday Americans could intensify in the months ahead, he added.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Job openings continue to dwindle in February: JOLTS report
  • Consumer confidence unexpectedly strengthens in March
  • Chicago PMI falls more than expected in March
  • Home-price growth decelerates in January: S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Home Price Index
  • FHFA house price index rises modestly in January

data.title: Aehr jumps after receiving order from new customer for data center market
data.description:
• Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) rose more than 13% on Tuesday after the semiconductor testing firm announced it had received an order from a “major new customer” for the data center market. 
  • The Fremont, Calif.-based Aehr said it had received the order from the unnamed customer, who is developing advanced silicon photonics-based transceivers for data center networking and optical I/O applications. Aehr will provide the customer with its FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in system, a fully integrated WaferPak Auto Aligner, multiple FOX-NP wafer-level burn-in systems, and multiple FOX WaferPak full-wafer contactors.
  • Aehr added that the customer has also provided a forecast for additional systems as soon as this calendar year.
  • “Data center architectures are rapidly evolving as AI infrastructure scales, driving a major transition from traditional copper interconnects to fiber optic communication links to meet the increasing demands for bandwidth, latency, signal integrity, thermal performance, and power efficiency,” said Aehr CEO and President Gayn Erickson in a statement. “Silicon photonics-based optical transceivers are a critical enabling technology for this transformation, and we believe this order is particularly significant because it comes from a major new customer that is a leading supplier of networking products and solutions for the data center market.”

data.title: Norwegian's conservative guidance shouldn't spook investors -- Stifel
data.description:
After three days in the red, weighed down by the volatility in the energy markets and the fluid geopolitical climate, shares of Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) are grinding higher, buoyed by a retreat in oil prices and an endorsement from Stifel.

In a note to clients after the company’s investor event, Stifel analyst Steven Wieczynski believes investors are incorrectly assuming management is “talking down yield guidance for the remainder of 2026."

“In our view, NOTHING (and we can’t say this loud enough) has changed versus when we met with [CEO John Chidsey] in mid-March,” Wieczynski says, noting that the company withheld yield guidance for the second half of this year to emphasize that Q3 will be the toughest from a yield perspective, with most of their inventory in weaker European and Alaskan markets.

“It absolutely makes sense for NCLH to be conservative [with FY26 guidance]. With a new CEO plus an activist on the scene, why not just write off 2026 and give the company time to focus on 2027, as nothing really can be done at this point in 2026 given the elongated booking window of the cruise industry?” Wieczynski says.

At issue is the company’s 2026 guidance  and comments surrounding pressure on pricing and yields. During the earnings call with analysts, CFO Mark Kempa noted that Norwegian entered 2026 slightly behind an ideal booking curve in certain itineraries, “creating near-term pressure on pricing and yield, which is evidenced in guidance.” Accordingly, Norwegian now expects its net yield to contract ~1.6% as higher occupancy is offset by pricing pressures. For the balance of the year, net yields are expected to stabilize and “modestly improve” by 0.6%, bringing full-year net yields to approximately flat.

As this conservatism likely spooked investors, Wieczynski believes this fueled a “significant dislocation” in the share price/valuation and that investors are overlooking two important factors in regard to NCLH. First, the company has adequate liquidity to de-lever the balance sheet and move closer to investment grade. And second, the core cruise customer/cruise industry has proven resilient during shocks to the system. 

“All told, we expect customer demand to again prove resilient…which in turn should allow the company to be in a steady state of meaningful profitability under most scenarios. Through that lens, we encourage investors to take advantage of the current market dislocation to accumulate shares for the long term,” Wieczynski advises.

Stifel maintains a Buy rating on Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) and a $28 price target, which represents a 59% upside to Monday’s closing price.

data.title: Regeneron a new overweight at Piper on Dupixent, pipeline
data.description:
Piper Sandler has initiated Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) at overweight saying that it expects continued growth in Dupixent (dupilumab) sales as well as "several high-potential pipeline assets."

The firm set a price target of $875 (~17% upside based on March 30 close).

Analyst Biren Amin is projecting Dupixent sales to surge ~50% from 2025-2030, reaching about ~$20B by the end of that period.

He noted that the company has done well to ameliorate competition from standard-dose Eylea (aflibercept) with Eylea HD, as the latter now accounts for 47% of the franchise's sales in the US. Amin added that loss of sales to biosimilars should be lessened by an anticipated Q2 approval of a prefilled syringe ("could serve as the next catalyst for REGN shares") and the recent approval of monthly dosing.

Amin called Regeneron's pipeline "underappreciated," emphasizing its Factor XI antibody program. He models peak risk-adjusted sales of (REGN7508/REGN9933) of $2.7B compared to the consensus of $1.1B. "By decoupling efficacy from bleeding risk, this next-generation anticoagulation approach has the potential to unlock the ~50% of the market currently untreated due to safety concerns."

Elsewhere in the pipeline, other potential long-term revenue drivers are "fianlimab in metastatic melanoma, cemidisiran in immunology, and cat/birch allergy programs."

data.title: Snap gains on report activist Irenic has taken a stake
data.description:
Snap Inc. (SNAP) jumped 8.5% on a report that activist investor Irenic Capital Management has taken a stake.

Irenic, which has an economic interest of ~2.5% in Snap's Class A shares, wants the company to spin off or shutter its smart glasses business, Specs, according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited a letter Irenic sent to Snap Chief Executive Officer and co-founder Evan Spiegel. The activist believes that moves Snap could make could boost its shares to more than $26 each.

The activist fund also wants Snap (SNAP) to rationalize its cost structure by cutting jobs  and changing its compensation structure for employees, according to the report. Irenic also is pushing Snap to concentrate on adopting artificial intelligence to improve ad monetization. Irenic also recommends a stock buyback with the shares so discounted. Snap shares have dropped more than 50% over the past year.

Michael Lynton, chairperson of Snap’s board responded to the report on Irenic to Bloomberg, saying the company has “taken steps to improve performance, strengthen free cash flow, and offset dilution, and will continue to evaluate actions that drive long-term value for all stockholders.”

data.title: Rising oil prices could indirectly affect consumer delinquencies as slower growth pressures unemployment – GS
data.description:
Rising oil prices (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) could indirectly affect consumer delinquencies, as slower economic growth may pressure unemployment upwards, thereby increasing the share of borrowers unable to service their debt, according to Goldman Sachs analysts.

While the direct impact of fuel costs (XB1:COM) on loan defaults remains limited, Ben Shumway, Goldman Sachs analyst, warned that risks are skewed to the downside should the Middle East conflict persist long enough to dampen growth in the second half of the year.

WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) prices have surged nearly 50% since the initial strikes in Iran, cascading into a nationwide increase of nearly $1 per gallon of gasoline.

Consumer sentiment has dropped 3.3 points month over month, bringing the University of Michigan index under its 2nd percentile value since the late 1970s. This reading is notably lower than levels seen during both the early 2000s tech bubble and the bottom of the Global Financial Crisis.

 

 

 

Nonetheless, the “direct transmission mechanism for oil price increases to impact consumer delinquencies remains weak,” the analyst explained.

Gasoline accounts for only 3.4% of the average U.S. household’s total expenditures, meaning even a 50% price increase sustained for six months would raise overall household spending by approximately 84 basis points—likely insufficient to trigger any meaningful rise in defaults.

Moreover, examining sector-specific trends, Shumway found little correlation between oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) and delinquency rates across subprime auto, unsecured consumer loans, and credit cards.

“Credit card delinquency changes appear to have no discernible trend or connection to oil prices,” he said, noting that past apparent correlations in subprime auto lending during the run-up to the Global Financial Crisis were likely driven by other macroeconomic factors rather than fuel costs.

So, despite the historic drop in consumer sentiment, the base case forecast remains optimistic.

The firm’s economists noted that “unemployment in the U.S. is now more resilient to oil-related shocks than it has been historically,” projecting unemployment will end the year at 4.6%, well within historical norms.

As a result, Goldman Sachs expects overall consumer delinquencies to be relatively unaffected by the current disruption in oil prices.

data.title: Perpetua Resources' $2.7B Export-Import Bank loan advanced to congressional notice
data.description:
Perpetua Resources (PPTA) up 7.6% in Tuesday's trading after saying the board of the Export-Import Bank of the U.S. agreed to notify Congress of a proposed $2.7B senior secured long-term loan for the development of its stibnite gold-antimony project in Idaho.

The move triggers a 25-day notice period to Congress and is the last formal step before the Ex-Im board will vote on final approval, which Perpetua (PPTA) said it expects soon after the notice period ends.

If the proposed loan is approved, Perpetua (PPTA) said it would have sufficient capital, together with $714M of cash on hand at year-end, to finance the estimated $2.57B capital cost to build the project.

In total, the company would receive a loan of $2.2B, after excluding capitalized interest and fees.

Perpetua (PPTA) also published updated project economics reflecting current commodity prices as well as year-end 2025 capital and operating cost estimates; assuming a $4,500/oz gold price, the updated model reports the project's unlevered, after-tax project net present value of $6.1B and after-tax internal rate of return of 32.3%, reaffirming the stibnite mine as a premier gold-antimony project.

data.title: Taco Bell is letting rewards members decide on a new menu item
data.description:
Taco Bell is asking U.S. customers to choose which international menu item will make its domestic debut through the company's first "Global Taco Vote."

Starting March 26, U.S. Rewards Members can vote daily in the Taco Bell app to decide between the Kickin' Chicken Taco from Thailand and the Butter Chicken Taco from India. The winning item will be available at U.S. locations for a limited time later this year.

The Kickin' Chicken Taco from Thailand features crispy chicken strips, lettuce, a three-cheese blend, pico de gallo, and a spicy sauce wrapped in a flour tortilla. It originally launched in 2019 to coincide with the brand's entry into the Thai market. Meanwhile, the butter chicken taco from India consists of slow-roasted chicken in a tomato cream-based sauce topped with onions, cilantro, lettuce, and cheddar cheese in a fried chalupa shell. The menu item was added in 2023 as part of a menu highlighting Indian culinary heritage.

"This vote is all about celebrating the creativity of Taco Bell menus around the world while bringing globally inspired flavors and innovations to U.S. fans," Amy Ellis Durini, chief marketing and strategy officer, Taco Bell International, said in the press release. "Both tacos have already built passionate followings in their home countries, and now we're giving U.S. fans the power to choose which one earns a spot on menus nationwide."

The voting period runs through April 1, and the winning menu option will be announced on April 2.

Taco Bell was spun off from PepsiCo (PEP) in 1997,  along with KFC and Pizza Hut, to form a new company, Tricon Global Restaurants, which evolved into Yum! Brands (YUM).

data.title: MiNK outlines initial ARDS Phase II data in H2 2026 while extending runway through 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MiNK Therapeutics (INKT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Dr. Jennifer Buell (President, CEO & Director) reiterated MiNK’s differentiation as “an off the shelf” allogeneic iNKT platform that is “administered without lymphodepletion, without HLA matching,” and said the company has “demonstrated clinical activity with a very favorable safety profile.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) highlighted oncology durability disclosed from the solid tumor program, citing “median overall survival exceeding 23 months” in combination with PD-1 therapies and “complete remissions extending beyond 2 years,” with the focus on checkpoint-refractory settings.
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) framed ARDS/hypoxemic pneumonia as the most near-term randomized catalyst, describing prior critically ill dosing experience and stating, “As a result of these findings, we are now well on our way to announcing the first dosing of patients in our randomized Phase II study that's designed to expand to a Phase II/III study,” with sites “planned to launch with our colleagues at top centers in Ukraine and in the U.S.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) emphasized non-dilutive funding and partnerships as a core operating model, including for the GvHD effort: “We have secured external funding to advance MiNK cells into graft-versus-host disease with the trial in the activation phase at University of Wisconsin.”
  • Melissa Orilall (Principal Financial Officer) said, “During 2025, we executed an at-the-market facility in a disciplined manner, ending the year with a cash balance of $13.4 million,” and added, “Since year-end, we have raised an additional $3 million through this program, extending our runway through 2026 and supporting key clinical milestones.”

Outlook

  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) guided to near-term clinical milestones rather than numerical financial guidance, saying ARDS/hypoxemic pneumonia dosing is “starting imminently” and that “we expect an initial clinical data in the second half of this year,” alongside initial data from GvHD “in the second half of the year.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) also pointed investors to multiple near-term updates: “in the first half of 2026… multiple data presentations and peer-reviewed publications,” and for MiNK-215, “we would expect to be announcing some within the next 3 to 5 months on our 215 program.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from a planned pulmonary launch “within weeks” to emphasizing imminent dosing and a defined timing for “initial clinical data in the second half of this year,” while continuing to position the pulmonary program as potentially expandable from Phase II to Phase II/III.

Financial Results

  • Melissa Orilall (Principal Financial Officer) reported a Q4 2025 net loss of $2.6 million, or $0.56 per share, and a full-year 2025 net loss of $12.5 million, or $2.93 per share.
  • Melissa Orilall (Principal Financial Officer) tied spending to pipeline execution, stating the results “reflect continued focused investment in advance in our agenT-797 clinical programs… while maintaining disciplined control over our operational spend.”
  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) characterized year-over-year cost and balance sheet changes, stating, “our cash increasing to about $13.4 million from $4.6 million and our operating cost decreasing nearly 40% over the course of the year.”

Q&A

  • Emily Bodnar, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about ARDS Phase II size and control arm and how IPF fits; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) said the control context is “standard of care” and “physicians' choice,” adding, “The cells will be added on top of standard of care,” while emphasizing MiNK iNKT activity “independent of steroids being on board” and that the program is led by “Dr. Terese Hammond.”
  • Emily Bodnar, H.C. Wainwright: Asked timing for second-line gastric cancer efficacy data; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) replied, “You will be seeing some efficacy data in the first half of this year at a major conference,” and said IPF planning updates are coming: “You're going to be hearing more about the design of that trial and the development path as we advance over the next couple of months.”
  • Mayank Mamtani, B. Riley: Asked about ARDS patient definition and differentiation, plus overlap with an IL-15 iNKT combination study appearing on clinicaltrials.gov; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) said ARDS enrollment will follow “a very specific global ARDS definition system,” with selection “based on their oxygenation,” and characterized current ARDS standard as “physician's choice,” while describing lung sampling and immune monitoring approaches.
  • Mayank Mamtani, B. Riley: Pressed on the IL-15 combination posting; Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) responded, “I want to be very clear that MiNK has not formally announced any collaboration or any clinical trials with the IL-15 superagonist,” and added, “The study that I've referenced is the MiNK-sponsored randomized Phase II trial that we have not yet posted on clinicaltrials.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly negative, with repeated pressure on trial design specifics and external disclosures, particularly around the ARDS control arm and the IL-15 combination posting on clinicaltrials.gov.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and clarifying, leaning on confidence in execution timing and defensive clarity on partnerships, including, “I want to be very clear that MiNK has not formally announced any collaboration.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management maintained an execution-focused tone, but the current call included more direct clarification around third-party trial listings and collaboration status, prompted by investor/analyst scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management continued emphasizing durability in refractory solid tumors and the pulmonary program as the next major value driver, but Q4 placed more emphasis on imminent randomized ARDS dosing, a global footprint (Ukraine and U.S.), and a timed expectation for “initial clinical data in the second half of this year.”
  • Financial leadership changed quarter-over-quarter: Q3 featured Christine Klaskin (Treasurer and Principal Financial & Accounting Officer), while Q4 introduced Melissa Orilall as newly appointed Principal Financial Officer.
  • Capital strategy messaging shifted from Q3’s cash balance of $14.3 million at quarter-end to Q4’s year-end cash of $13.4 million plus “an additional $3 million” raised since year-end, with continued emphasis on extending runway “through 2026.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) underscored ARDS clinical and operational complexity, describing severely ill patients and ICU settings, while positioning practicality as mitigation: “We can get the cells into patients in community hospitals.”
  • Partnership and disclosure risk surfaced in Q&A, where management addressed market confusion directly: “MiNK has not formally announced any collaboration” related to the IL-15 superagonist.
  • Funding discipline remained a stated constraint for expansion into IPF, with Dr. Buell (President, CEO & Director) saying MiNK will be “very responsible… about how we're going to be funding that program.”

Final Takeaway

MiNK management centered the 2026 investment narrative on near-term randomized execution in ARDS/hypoxemic pneumonia, with first dosing described as imminent and “initial clinical data” expected in the second half of the year, alongside GvHD trial activation and dosing supported by non-dilutive funding. Management also highlighted updated durability signals in checkpoint-refractory solid tumors and positioned capital efficiency as core, citing year-end cash of $13.4 million, an additional $3 million raised since year-end, and runway “through 2026,” while using the Q&A to explicitly clarify that MiNK “has not formally announced any collaboration” tied to an IL-15 superagonist trial listing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500 stocks with biggest Y/Y jump in fund ownership breadth - BofA
data.description:
Artificial intelligence-related stocks (AIEQ) are dominating the list of S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) (VOO) names seeing the biggest increase in fund ownership breadth over the past year, according to a new Bank of America Research screen.

Palantir Technologies (PLTR) experienced the most dramatic jump, with fund ownership climbing to 32% from just 8% a year ago. The data intelligence company has emerged as a clear favorite among institutional investors expanding their AI exposure.

The screen also highlighted GE Vernova (GEV), Amphenol (APH), Western Digital (WDC), Broadcom (AVGO), AMD (AMD) and Arista Networks (ANET) alongside Meta (META) as names attracting broader fund participation. Notably, more managers are adding these stocks to their portfolios rather than simply increasing existing position sizes. Gilead Sciences (GLD) and McKesson (MCK) were also in the mix.

The composition of the list reveals how the AI investment thesis has evolved beyond semiconductor demand. Fund managers are now targeting companies across power equipment, data infrastructure, connectivity and enterprise software as direct beneficiaries of the technology shift.

This expanding roster of AI-adjacent plays helps explain why the trade has maintained momentum even as valuations in some corners of the technology sector have grown difficult to justify. For institutional investors, the opportunity set continues to widen as AI applications reach deeper into the economy.
